class_schedule.topic: From Product Design to Service Design
learning_objectives: Distinguish service design from product/UX design in scope and methods
Identify the components of a service: touchpoints, channels, frontstage, backstage
Map the service ecosystem including actors, processes, and technology
readings: This Is Service Design Doing by Stickdorn et al., Ch. 1-3
Service Design for Business by Reason, Lovlie & Flu, Ch. 1

lecture_notes.introduction: Service design extends UX beyond individual screens to the entire end-to-end experience across all touchpoints — digital, physical, and human. It designs the system behind the experience.
key_concepts: Service design vs. UX design: holistic experience across all channels
Frontstage (customer-facing) vs. backstage (operations) design
Service touchpoints: website, app, phone call, physical store, email, packaging
Service blueprinting as the core service design deliverable
The service-dominant logic: value is co-created with customers
lecture_notes.demonstration: Mapping the complete service experience of ordering a prescription — from doctor visit through pharmacy website, in-store pickup, and refill notification
setup_requirements: Large whiteboard or Miro board for service mapping

review.value: Frontstage is everything the customer sees and interacts with. Backstage is the processes, people, and systems that enable the frontstage experience — both must be designed together.
A diagram showing the customer journey, frontstage actions, backstage processes, and support systems in aligned rows — revealing how internal operations affect customer experience.

learning_objectives: Create comprehensive service blueprints with all five layers
Identify failure points and bottlenecks in service processes
Use blueprints to align cross-functional teams around the customer experience
readings: This Is Service Design Doing by Stickdorn et al., Ch. 5
Service Blueprinting: A Practical Technique (NNg article)

lecture_notes.introduction: Service blueprints are the most powerful tool in the service designer's toolkit. They reveal the invisible systems behind customer experiences and identify where breakdowns occur.
key_concepts: Five blueprint layers: customer actions, frontstage employee actions, backstage employee actions, support processes, physical evidence
The line of interaction, line of visibility, and line of internal interaction
Failure points: where the service commonly breaks down
Wait points: where customers experience delays
Moment of truth: make-or-break moments in the service experience
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a detailed service blueprint for a hotel check-in experience — from online booking through room entry

review.value: The boundary between what customers can see (frontstage) and what they cannot (backstage). Everything above the line shapes perception; everything below enables it.
Marked with red indicators on the blueprint — moments where the service commonly fails. Each needs a designed recovery path.

learning_objectives: Design omnichannel service experiences with seamless channel transitions
Map service journeys across time including pre-service, service, and post-service
Identify and design for moments of truth that define the service experience
readings: Mapping Experiences by Jim Kalbach, Ch. 5-7
Outside In by Harley Manning & Kerry Bodine, Ch. 3-5

lecture_notes.introduction: Modern services span multiple channels and extend over time. Orchestrating this journey across digital and physical touchpoints is the core challenge of service design.
key_concepts: Omnichannel design: seamless transitions between digital and physical
Service journey phases: awareness, consideration, purchase, onboarding, use, support, advocacy
Channel-appropriate design: matching interaction to channel strengths
Recovery design: turning service failures into loyalty opportunities
The service recovery paradox: well-handled failures build loyalty
lecture_notes.demonstration: Designing the omnichannel journey for returning a product — starting online, printing a label, dropping at a physical location, and receiving the refund
setup_requirements: Journey mapping template with channel lanes

review.value: Multichannel means present on many channels. Omnichannel means all channels share context and state. The customer should not have to repeat information when switching channels.
A customer who experiences a well-handled service failure can become more loyal than one who never had a problem — effective recovery design is a competitive advantage.

learning_objectives: Map service stakeholders and their relationships, incentives, and constraints
Design organizational structures that enable good service
Address misaligned incentives between departments that harm customer experience
readings: Good Services by Lou Downe, Ch. 5-7
Org Design for Design Orgs by Merholz & Skinner, Ch. 3-4

lecture_notes.introduction: Service quality depends on organizational design. The best-designed customer journey fails if internal silos, misaligned incentives, or broken processes undermine delivery.
key_concepts: Stakeholder mapping: identifying all actors in the service ecosystem
Incentive alignment: when department KPIs conflict with customer experience
Organizational silos and their impact on cross-channel service
Service ownership: who is responsible for the end-to-end experience
Change management: introducing service design into organizations
lecture_notes.demonstration: Stakeholder mapping for a healthcare service — identifying how insurance, hospital, pharmacy, and patient incentives align and conflict

review.value: Call center KPIs measure call duration (efficiency) while satisfaction requires thorough help (effectiveness). These conflicting metrics produce bad service.
When no one owns the end-to-end experience, gaps form between channels. Service design advocates for a journey owner with cross-department authority.

learning_objectives: Prototype service experiences using desktop walkthroughs and role-playing
Design service pilots that test the complete experience at small scale
Measure service quality using appropriate service metrics
readings: This Is Service Design Doing by Stickdorn et al., Ch. 7-8
Sprint by Jake Knapp, adapted for service prototyping

lecture_notes.introduction: Services cannot be prototyped like digital products. Service prototyping requires creative techniques from desktop walkthroughs to full-scale pilots with real customers.
key_concepts: Desktop walkthrough: miniature figures acting out the service on a tabletop
Role-playing: team members acting as customers and employees
Service pilots: small-scale real-world tests with real customers
Service metrics: NPS, CSAT, CES (Customer Effort Score), time-to-resolution
Iterating services: changing people and processes, not just code
lecture_notes.demonstration: Running a desktop walkthrough of a restaurant reservation and dining service with class members playing various roles
setup_requirements: Miniature figures and props for desktop walkthrough

review.value: A rapid service prototyping technique using miniature figures on a tabletop to walk through the service step by step — reveals process gaps and bottlenecks in minutes.
Measures how much effort the customer had to expend. Lower effort correlates more strongly with loyalty than high satisfaction.

questions.question: A customer calls a bank to dispute a charge. They are transferred three times, repeat their account number each time, and wait 15 minutes. Using service design concepts, identify the root causes and propose a systemic fix.
questions.answer: Root causes: (1) Organizational silos — each transfer crosses a department boundary where context is lost. (2) No service ownership — nobody owns the end-to-end dispute journey. (3) Missing backstage integration — CRM does not pass customer context between agents. Systemic fix: implement shared CRM passing interaction context, design a blueprint eliminating unnecessary transfers, assign a journey owner, and add IVR pre-qualification for correct initial routing.

rubric.key: Identifies organizational silos as root cause
Proposes systemic fix addressing root causes

questions.question: Describe the five layers of a service blueprint and the three lines that separate them. Explain why each line matters.
questions.answer: Five layers: (1) Physical evidence, (2) Customer actions, (3) Frontstage employee actions, (4) Backstage employee actions, (5) Support processes. Three lines: Line of interaction (between customer and frontstage employee — where the customer touches the service), Line of visibility (between frontstage and backstage — what customers can vs. cannot see), Line of internal interaction (between backstage employees and support systems). Each line matters because it marks a boundary where information, context, or experience can be lost — designing for smooth handoff across these boundaries is the core purpose of service blueprinting.

rubric.key: Explains why each line matters for service quality

questions.question: Explain the difference between multichannel and omnichannel service design with a specific retail return example for each.
questions.answer: Multichannel: the retailer offers returns via website, in-store, and phone — but each operates independently. The customer starts online, gets a case number, goes to the store where the associate cannot look up the online case and asks them to start over. Omnichannel: all channels share state. The customer starts online, gets a QR code, the in-store associate scans it and sees the full history — no repetition needed. The key difference is context persistence across channels.

questions.question: A company has high CSAT (4.2/5) but low NPS (score of 15). What might explain this and what service design investigation would you conduct?
questions.answer: The discrepancy suggests satisfaction with individual interactions but unwillingness to recommend overall. Possible explanations: (1) Service frequently fails but recovery is good — customers appreciate the fix but resent needing it. (2) Individual touchpoints work but total journey effort is high. Investigation: map the end-to-end journey for cumulative friction, measure Customer Effort Score, analyze repeat contact rates, and interview NPS detractors about their full journey.

major_assignments.title: Service Redesign Project
major_assignments.description: Select a real service with known experience problems. Conduct service research, create a current-state service blueprint, identify improvement opportunities, design a future-state service blueprint, and prototype one key improvement.
objectives: Research a real service from both customer and employee perspectives
Create current-state service blueprints documenting pain points
Design future-state service improvements addressing root causes
Prototype and test a key improvement
deliverables: Service research report: customer interviews, employee interviews, mystery shopping
Current-state service blueprint with annotated pain points
Future-state service blueprint with prioritized improvements
Service prototype: desktop walkthrough or small-scale pilot
Improvement measurement plan with proposed metrics

criteria: Both customer and employee perspectives captured
Multiple research methods used
Pain points supported by evidence
Backstage processes investigated
All five layers accurately represented
Failure points and wait points identified
Cross-channel journey captured
Root causes analyzed, not just symptoms
Improvements address identified root causes
Feasibility considered for each improvement
Both frontstage and backstage changes specified
Prioritization based on impact and effort
Prototype tests the most impactful improvement
Stakeholders participated in walkthrough or pilot
Findings documented with iteration plans
Measurement plan uses appropriate service metrics
Thorough research from customer and employee perspectives
Accurate current-state blueprint with root cause analysis
Feasible future-state improvements
Tested prototype with measurement plan

weekly_quizzes.description: Quizzes on service design theory, blueprinting, and journey orchestration

service_labs.description: Weekly labs practicing service blueprinting, journey mapping, and stakeholder analysis

service_redesign_project.description: End-to-end service redesign with research, blueprinting, and prototyping

service_critiques.description: Weekly analysis of real-world services evaluating design quality

final_presentation.description: Presentation of service redesign to stakeholder panel

course_policies.attendance: Workshop sessions are mandatory as they involve collaborative service mapping and role-playing. More than 2 absences affect grades.
course_policies.late_submissions: Service research phases have sequential deadlines. Late work penalized 10% per day.
course_policies.collaboration: Service research may involve paired interviews. All blueprints and design must be individual work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All research must be conducted with real customers and employees with informed consent. Fabricating data results in course failure.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Miro for service blueprinting, Figma for digital touchpoint design, audio recording for interviews, and presentation software.
